steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The researchers conclude that net CO₂ is likely to increase when snow arrives early (before soil freezes). A supporting finding must explain the causal mechanism: why early snow would cause more CO₂ output than uptake during that window.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'The effect of soil temperature on vegetation growth and heterotrophic respiration is stronger during snow cover than without.': This says both processes are amplified during snow cover equally — it doesn't show why one would dominate the other to produce net CO₂ increase. ✗
  • Choice B — 'Early snow reduces soil moisture for plant growth and lowers heterotrophic respiration.': This reduces both processes, giving no clear reason why net CO₂ would increase — if respiration is also reduced, the balance is unclear. ✗
  • Choice C — 'Snow cover persists longer in areas of low vegetation growth and high heterotrophic respiration.': This describes a geographic pattern, not a temporal mechanism explaining why early snow causes net CO₂ increase. ✗
  • Choice D — 'Snow insulation allows heterotrophic respiration to continue during a period when plants are not growing.': This directly explains the mechanism: early snow insulates the soil so microorganisms keep releasing CO₂, but plants aren't yet active to absorb it — CO₂ output goes uncompensated, producing a net increase. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
